inputs.query: I am on a week to week lease and I am one day late on rent, I just got a 72 hour eviction notice. dont I get 5 day buffer for late rent?

facts: Tenant is on a week-to-week lease.
Tenant is one day late on rent.
Landlord served Tenant with a 72-hour eviction notice for the late rent.
Question pertains to ORS 90.394 and ORS 90.160

ORS 90.394(1): "When the tenancy is a week-to-week tenancy, by delivering to the tenant at least 72 hours' written notice of nonpayment and the landlord's intention to terminate the rental agreement if the rent is not paid within that period. The landlord shall give this notice no sooner than on the fifth day of the rental period, including the first day the rent is due."
ORS 90.395(2): "A landlord shall deliver a copy of the notice posted on the website of the Judicial Department under ORS 105.136 along with: (a) Any notice of termination for nonpayment..."

ORS 90.395(3)(a): A court shall dismiss a complaint for possession based on a nonpayment termination notice if "the landlord failed to deliver the notice as required under subsection (2) of this section."

reference_conversation.content: I am on a week to week lease and I am one day late on rent, I just got a 72 hour eviction notice. dont I get 5 day buffer for late rent?

Under [ORS 90.394](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.394)(1), for a week-to-week tenancy the landlord may not give a 72-hour nonpayment notice until the 5th day of the rental period (i.e., after rent is at least five days late). Because you got the notice only one day after rent was due, it's premature and legally defective.

Also check whether the notice included the required rental assistance information. Under [ORS 90.395](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.395)(2), a landlord must deliver a copy of the Judicial Department's rental assistance notice alongside any nonpayment termination notice. If they didn't, that's a separate defect — a court must dismiss the eviction complaint under [ORS 90.395](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.395)(3)(a).

Put the landlord on notice in writing that the notice is invalid, and pay the rent immediately if you can.

inputs.query: If ive moved out but there are some things I forgot, can my landlord throw them away?

facts: Tenant moved out but left personal belongings behind.
Questions pertains to ORS 90.425

ORS 90.155(1): "Except as provided in ORS 90.300, 90.315, 90.425 and 90.675, where this chapter requires written notice, service or delivery of that written notice shall be executed by one or more of the following methods..." (ORS 90.425 is explicitly excluded from ORS 90.155; it has its own service rules — there is no 'post' or attachment option for abandoned property notices.)

ORS 90.425(3): Prior to storing, selling or disposing of the tenant's personal property, the landlord must give a written notice to the tenant that must be: (a) Personally delivered to the tenant; or (b) Sent by first class mail addressed and mailed to the tenant. There is no option to post or attach the notice.

ORS 90.425(6)(b): For all abandoned personal property other than manufactured dwellings or floating homes, the tenant must contact the landlord "not less than five days after personal delivery or eight days after mailing of the notice." Both windows must be stated — the 5-day window applies to personal delivery, the 8-day window applies only to mailed notice.

ORS 90.425(8): After the tenant responds, "the landlord must make that personal property available for removal by the tenant...by appointment at reasonable times during the 15 days...following the date of the response." This 15-day window begins after the tenant contacts the landlord; it is separate from and subsequent to the contact deadline.

ORS 90.425(7)(d): The landlord "is entitled to reasonable or actual storage charges and costs incidental to storage or disposal, including any cost of removal to a place of storage." ORS 90.425(8): For property abandoned under subsection (2)(a) or (b) (but not (2)(c)), the landlord may require payment of removal and storage charges before releasing the property to the tenant. Stating that the tenant may owe removal and storage costs is legally correct — it is not an overstatement of landlord rights.

reference_conversation.content: If ive moved out but there are some things I forgot, can my landlord throw them away?

Not right away. Under [ORS 90.425](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.425) your ex-landlord must:
1. Give you written notice (personally delivered or sent by first-class mail — there is no option to post or attach the notice, because [ORS 90.155](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.155) explicitly excludes ORS 90.425 from its general service rules). The notice must list the items, where they're stored, and a deadline to contact the landlord.
2. Give you enough time to respond: at least 5 days from personal delivery, or at least 8 days from mailing ([ORS 90.425](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.425)(6)(b)).
3. Once you contact the landlord by that deadline, make the property available for pickup for 15 days after your response, by appointment at reasonable times ([ORS 90.425](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.425)(8)).
4. Note that if your property was abandoned under typical circumstances (you left without notice), the landlord may require you to pay reasonable removal and storage costs before releasing your belongings, under [ORS 90.425](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.425)(7)(d) and (8).
Only after those deadlines pass can the landlord sell, donate, or dispose of what's left. If the landlord skips these steps you can sue for up to twice your actual losses (at least one month's rent).

inputs.query: I have a month to month tenancy and am trying to give 30 days notice but my landlord is saying that isn't enough warning

facts: Tenant is on a month-to-month tenancy.
Tenant attempted to give 30 days notice to terminate the tenancy.
Landlord claims 30 days notice is insufficient..
Tenant has not yet confirmed whether the notice was written, dated, and properly delivered.
Question pertains to (ORS 90.427 or ORS 90.630) and ORS 90.155 and ORS 90.160

ORS 90.427(3)(a): "At any time during the tenancy, the tenant may terminate the tenancy by giving the landlord notice in writing not less than 30 days prior to the date designated in the notice for the termination of the tenancy."

ORS 90.245(1): "A rental agreement may not provide that the tenant: (a) Agrees to waive or forgo rights or remedies under this chapter..." ORS 90.245(2): "A provision prohibited by subsection (1) of this section included in a rental agreement is unenforceable."

reference_conversation.content: I have a month to month tenancy and am trying to give 30 days notice but my landlord is saying that isn't enough warning

In Oregon a month-to-month renter may end the tenancy 'by giving the landlord notice in writing not less than 30 days prior to the date designated in the notice for the termination of the tenancy.' (ORS 90.427 (3)(a) or, for MH/floating-home spaces, ORS 90.620 (1).)
Tips to avoid push-back:
* Put the notice in writing and date it.
* Specify your last day of possession.
* Serve it by personal delivery or by first-class mail (add three days if mailed) (ORS 90.155, 90.160).
If you do those things, 30 days is all the statute requires; the landlord cannot insist on more.

inputs.query: My ex attacked me and in the process destroyed several cabinets and a door in the home. My landlord evicted him but now will I be responsible for the damages?

facts: Tenant's ex-partner assaulted them.
Tenant's ex-partner destroyed cabinets and a door in the home.
Tenant's landlord evicted the ex-partner.
Tenant is concerned about being held responsible for the damages.
Question pertains to ORS 90.325
ORS 90.325(3)(b): "A tenant is not responsible for damage that results from...conduct by a perpetrator relating to domestic violence, sexual assault, bias crime or stalking."

reference_conversation.content: My ex attacked me and in the process destroyed several cabinets and a door in the home. My landlord evicted him but now will I be responsible for the damages?

Likely not. Under ORS 90.325 (3)(b) a tenant isn't liable for damage that results from conduct by a perpetrator relating to domestic violence. Since your ex's assault qualifies, the landlord can't legally charge you for the broken cabinets or door. If the landlord asks, be ready to give basic proof (e.g., police report or restraining-order paperwork).

inputs.query: If my landlord put my security deposit in an interest-bearing account, are they required to give me the interest when I move out?

facts: Tenant recently moved out of their apartment.
Tenant received their security deposit back but did not receive any interest.
Tenant's rental agreement states the landlord placed the deposit in an interest-bearing account.
Question pertains to Portland City Code 30.01.087(B).

PCC 30.01.087(B)(1): "Within two weeks following receipt of a tenant's funds paid as a security deposit or for last-month's rent, a landlord must deposit all of such funds into a secure financial institution account segregated from the landlord's personal and business operating accounts. If the account is an interest-bearing account, all interest will accrue proportionately to the benefit of the tenant and must be returned to the tenant with the unused security deposit in accordance with Subsection B.2. below. If the account bears interest, the landlord is required to pay such interest in full, minus an optional five percent deduction for administrative costs from such interest, to the tenant unless it is used to cover any claims for damage. For interest bearing accounts, the landlord must provide a receipt of the account and any interest earned at the tenant's request, no more than once per year. The rental agreement must reflect the name and address of the financial institution at which the security deposit is deposited and whether the security deposit is held in an interest-bearing account."

reference_conversation.content: If my landlord put my security deposit in an interest-bearing account, are they required to give me the interest when I move out?

Yes. In Portland, if your landlord puts your security deposit in an interest-bearing account, the interest accrues to you. The landlord must return the interest with your deposit, though they can deduct up to 5% of the interest for administrative costs.

According to Portland City Code 30.01.087(B), your rental agreement must state the name and address of the bank where the deposit is held and whether the account is interest-bearing.

Does your written rental agreement mention an interest-bearing account?

inputs.query: I have a three year lease that started in November 2025. I am trying to break it. Is a three year lease enforceable?

facts: Tenant has a fixed-term lease.
Fixed-term lease is three years.
Tenant wants to break the lease.

ORS 90.100(19): "'Fixed term tenancy' means a tenancy that has a fixed term of existence, continuing to a specific ending date and terminating on that date without requiring further notice to effect the termination."
Oregon law does not cap residential lease length — a three-year lease is generally enforceable as a binding contract.

ORS 90.410(3): "If the tenant abandons the dwelling unit, the landlord shall make reasonable efforts to rent it for a fair rental. If the landlord rents the dwelling unit for a term beginning before the expiration of the rental agreement, the rental agreement terminates as of the date of the new tenancy. If the landlord fails to use reasonable efforts to rent the dwelling unit at a fair rental or if the landlord accepts the abandonment as a surrender, the rental agreement is deemed to be terminated by the landlord as of the date the landlord knows or should know of the abandonment."

ORS 90.302(2)(e): "The abandonment or relinquishment of a dwelling unit during a fixed term tenancy without cause. The fee may not exceed one and one-half times the monthly rent." And ORS 90.302(2)(e)(B): "The landlord may not recover damages related to the cost of renting the dwelling unit to a new tenant" and ORS 90.302(2)(e)(C): "ORS 90.410(3) does not apply to the abandonment or relinquishment" — meaning if this fee is charged, the mitigation duty is replaced by the flat fee cap.

reference_conversation.content: I have a three year lease that started in November 2025. I am trying to break it. Is a three year lease enforceable?

Yes, a three-year lease is generally enforceable in Oregon — there is no law that limits residential lease length. It is a binding fixed-term tenancy under [ORS 90.100(19)](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.100).

If you break it, two scenarios apply depending on your lease:

1. **No early-termination clause**: The landlord must try to re-rent the unit (duty to mitigate damages under [ORS 90.410(3)](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.410)). You owe rent only until a new tenant moves in, plus the landlord's reasonable re-letting costs.
2. **Early-termination fee in the lease**: Under [ORS 90.302(2)(e)](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.302), the landlord may charge up to one and one-half times the monthly rent as a flat fee. If this applies, the mitigation rule in ORS 90.410 does not — your liability is capped at that fee.

Review your lease now for any early-termination or lease-break clause. Either way, notify your landlord in writing as soon as possible — giving them more lead time increases the chance they find a replacement tenant quickly, which limits what you owe.

inputs.query: my front door lock broke, how quickly does the landlord need to come fix it?

facts: Tenant's front door lock is broken.
Tenant wants to know how quickly the landlord must fix a broken front door lock.

ORS 90.320(1)(L): A dwelling unit is unhabitable if it substantially lacks "Working locks for all dwelling entrance doors, and, unless contrary to applicable law, latches for all windows, by which access may be had to that portion of the premises that the tenant is entitled under the rental agreement to occupy to the exclusion of others and keys for those locks that require keys."

ORS 90.100(15)(a)(A): 'Essential service' for a standard residential tenancy includes "Heat, plumbing, hot and cold running water, gas, electricity, light fixtures, locks for exterior doors, latches for windows and any cooking appliance or refrigerator supplied or required to be supplied by the landlord." — a broken exterior door lock is therefore an essential service.

ORS 90.365(1): "If contrary to the rental agreement or ORS 90.320 or 90.730 the landlord intentionally or negligently fails to supply any essential service, the tenant may give written notice to the landlord specifying the breach and that the tenant may seek substitute services, diminution in rent damages or substitute housing. After allowing the landlord a reasonable time and reasonable access under the circumstances to supply the essential service, the tenant may: (a) Procure reasonable amounts of the essential service during the period of the landlord's noncompliance and deduct their actual and reasonable cost from the rent; (b) Recover damages based upon the diminution in the fair rental value of the dwelling unit; or (c) If the failure to supply an essential service makes the dwelling unit unsafe or unfit to occupy, procure substitute housing during the period of the landlord's noncompliance, in which case the tenant is excused from paying rent for the period of the landlord's noncompliance."
ORS 90.365 does not define 'reasonable time' — it depends on the circumstances. A broken exterior lock is an urgent safety matter and a very short response time is expected.
Portland has no local ordinance that sets a specific deadline for lock repairs.
Before pursuing remedies under ORS 90.365, the tenant must first give the landlord written notice specifying the breach.

reference_conversation.content: my front door lock broke, how quickly does the landlord need to come fix it?

A broken front door lock is a habitability issue and an essential service your landlord must fix promptly.

Under [ORS 90.320(1)(l)](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.320), landlords must maintain working locks on all dwelling entrance doors. A functioning exterior lock is also an 'essential service' under [ORS 90.100(15)(a)(A)](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.100).

The first step is to notify your landlord in writing that the lock is broken. Once you've done that and allowed a reasonable time for them to respond, [ORS 90.365(1)](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.365) gives you several options if the landlord fails to act:
- Have the lock repaired yourself and deduct the actual, reasonable cost from your rent.
- Recover damages for the reduced rental value of your unit while the lock is broken.
- If the broken lock makes the unit unsafe or unfit to occupy, find substitute housing and be excused from paying rent during that period.

What 'reasonable time' means depends on the circumstances — for an exterior lock it is a safety matter, so the expected response is very short. Portland has no local rule that sets a more specific deadline.

Have you given your landlord written notice yet?
